elements.sent-2411-001: The Schrieffer-Wolff transformation (SWT) is an important perturbative method in quantum mechanics used to simplify Hamiltonians by decoupling low- and high-energy subspaces.
elements.sent-2411-002: Existing methods for implementing the SWT often lack general applicability to arbitrary perturbative systems or fail to provide a closed-form solution for the SWT generator.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this article, we present a systematic and unified framework for the SWT that addresses these shortcomings.
elements.sent-2411-004: Specifically, we derive a closed-form solution for the SWT generator that is universally applicable to any system that satisfies the conditions required to be perturbatively treated.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, we extend this solution to time-dependent systems with periodic perturbations, covering all frequency regimes.
elements.sent-2411-006: The effectiveness of this approach is then demonstrated by applying it to analyze the dispersive shift of an anharmonic resonator coupled to a two-level system with time-dependent coupling.
elements.sent-2411-007: The Schrieffer-Wolff transformation (SWT) is a widely utilized perturbation theory approach for simplifying the analysis of complex quantum systems.
elements.sent-2411-008: Unlike traditional time-independent perturbation theory, which focuses on computing corrections to the energy levels of a system, the SWT transforms the original Hamiltonian into an effective low-energy Hamiltonian by eliminating interactions between disparate energy subspaces.
elements.sent-2411-009: This makes the SWT particularly advantageous for studying problems in condensed matter physics~\cite{SW_effective_hopping_in_silicene,SW_fermi_hubbard_model,SW_ising_quantum_criticality,SW_kondo_lattice,SW_mott_insulators,SW_superconductor_physics,SW_quantum_lattice_models,SW_superconductor_physics_1,SW_superconductive_physics_2,SW_XXZ_chain , quantum optics~\cite{SW_chain_coupled_to_cavity,SW_dissipative_bosonic_modes,SW_microwave_theory,SW_optical_lattice_interacting_bosons,SW_spin_squeezed_resonator , quantum information sciences~\cite{SW_electrons_in_helium,SW_anomalous_zero_field_splitting, SW_electrical_control_of_hole_qubits,SW_beyond_dispersive_regime,SW_cavity_control_of_spin_qubits,SW_dispersive_two_qubit_gates,SW_ultrafast_Ge_qubits,SW_emergent_linear_rashba_in_holes,SW_entanglement_generation,SW_flopping_mode_qubits,SW_hole_spin_qubits_in_Si_FinFETs,SW_hole_spin_qubits_in_wells,SW_Light_hole_spin_qubit,SW_on_IBM_quantum_computer,SW_photodriven_germanium_hole_qubit,SW_quantum_algorithms,SW_quantum_algorithms_1,SW_quantum_computation_protocol,SW_quantum_information_processing,SW_quantum_phase_estimation_algorithm,SW_recent_advances_in_hole_spin_qubits,SW_role_of_anisotropic_confining_of_Ge_qubit,SW_super_conducting_qubits,SW_transferring_entangled_states as well as several other fields~\cite{SW_dissipative_systems,SW_dynamic_nuclear_polarization,SW_krein_hermitian_hamiltonians,SW_non_hermitian_systems,SW_scar_states , where the primary interest lies in understanding the low-energy behavior of the studied systems.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: One of the key reasons for employing the SWT over other methods of perturbation theory is its ability to retain the operator structure of the Hamiltonian, thereby providing a more physical understanding of how perturbations affect the system.
elements.sent-2411-011: This contrasts with traditional approaches that mainly yield shifts to the original energy levels.
elements.sent-2411-012: It is also well known that upon second order, the SWT returns the results obtained by regular perturbation theory approaches.
elements.sent-2411-013: Additionally, slight alterations of the generally accepted SWT~\cite{SW_MultiblockDiagonalization , seem to suggest that this equivalence could be extended to higher orders for a variety of systems.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The implementation of the SWT has been approached through a variety of methods~\cite{SW_M_Andersson_impurity_model,SW_M_Andersson_in_superconductor,SW_M_Hubbard_models,SW_M_interacting_picture_quasi_eigen,SW_M_Kondo_Model,SW_M_many_body,SW_M_underscreened_andersson_lattice, SW_RecursiveSW , most of which aim to construct a generator for the unitary transformation that decouples the high-energy and low-energy subspaces of a given Hamiltonian.}
elements.sent-2411-015: These methods can broadly be categorized into two classes: operator-level approaches and matrix-element-based approaches.
elements.sent-2411-016: In the operator approach, originally introduced by Schrieffer and Wolff~\cite{SW_original_paper_on_SW , the generator is typically found by solving an operator equation that relates the unperturbed Hamiltonian to the perturbation.}
elements.sent-2411-017: This method preserves the operator structure, thus offering valuable physical insights.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, this often relies on heuristic assumptions that make it difficult to generalize: this is especially true for infinite-dimensional systems where determining the generator can be particularly challenging.
elements.sent-2411-019: In contrast, matrix-element-based approaches systematically sum over the interactions between the states of the system, providing a more procedural and straightforward path to deriving the effective Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-020: However, these approaches sacrifice some of the intuitive understanding provided by the operator methods, as the focus shifts from the operator-level dynamics to the energy levels.
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, matrix-element methods encounter significant difficulties when dealing with infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, as they inherently require truncation of these spaces, which compromises their ability to accurately capture the system's dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-022: Despite the extensive literature on the SWT, most existing methods fail to provide a close-form solution that is applicable to any system.
elements.sent-2411-023: Rather, they tend to offer reformulations that simplify the process of finding the generator in specific cases, without addressing the need for a universally applicable solution.
elements.sent-2411-024: One notable exception is the work presented in~\cite{2021_paper_from_hell , where the authors offer a general solution to the SWT generator that is applicable to a broad class of systems.}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, their approach is limited to time independent systems with either purely bosonic or purely fermionic Hilbert spaces and does not account for interactions between finite and infinite-dimensional subspaces.
elements.sent-2411-026: The primary objective of this article is to overcome the limitations of current SWT implementations by presenting a universal solution for the SWT generator that can be applied to any perturbative system.
elements.sent-2411-027: To achieve this, we propose a unified framework that integrates the operator and matrix-element approaches into a cohesive methodology.
elements.sent-2411-028: This approach enables the construction of effective Hamiltonians while preserving operator-level insights, eliminating the need for heuristic assumptions or truncation of the total Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-029: Additionally, we extend the this solution to time-dependent systems, offering a universal formalism applicable to a wide range of scenarios encountered in the study of quantum systems.
elements.sent-2411-030: In this section we provide an introduction to the most widely accepted description of the SWT~\cite{SW_wikler_book .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Due to the large amount of literature covering the topic, we here provide only those details required to understand our main results.
elements.sent-2411-032: Consider a system described by a Hamiltonian $\mathcal{H}$ that can be partitioned into two parts \begin{equation}H = \sum_{i=0}H^{(i)} + \sum_{j=1} V^{(j)}, \label{eq: separated hamiltonian}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{H ^{(0)}$ represents the unperturbed Hamiltonian of the system, whose solutions are known.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Therefore, $\mathcal{H ^{(0)}$ is assumed to have a spectrum of eigenstates $\{\ket{n}\}$, associated with energies $\{E_n\}$, which are here assumed to contain a low-energy susbpace (spanned by states $\{\ket{L}\}$) and a high-energy subspace (spanned by $\{|H\rangle\}$).}
elements.sent-2411-034: These are then coupled to eachother via the perturbative interaction terms $V^{(j)}$ of order $j$.
elements.sent-2411-035: The remaining $\mathcal{H ^{(i)}$ terms in Eq.~(\ref{eq: separated hamiltonian ) can then be interpreted as the diagonal blocks defining the preturbative dynamics of order $i$, within the $\{\ket{L}\}$ and $\{\ket{H}\}$ subspaces.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The objective of the standard SWT is to construct an effective Hamiltonian $\mathcal{H _{\text{eff }$ that acts only within the low-energy subspace, encapsulating the influence of the high-energy states without explicitly involving them.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The transformation is constructed via a unitary operator $ U = e^{-S $, where $S$ is an anti-Hermitian operator, $S^\dagger = -S$, chosen to eliminate the coupling between the $\{|L\rangle\}$ and $\{|H\rangle\}$ subspaces to desired order in the perturbation $\sum_{j V^{(j)}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The transformed Hamiltonian is expressed as \begin{equation}H_eff = U H U^\dagger = e^{-S} H e^{S}. \label{eq: complete time independent rotation}\end{equation} Using the Baker-Campbell-Haussdorf formula we expand the exponential as the series \begin{equation}H_eff = H + [H, S] + \frac{1}{2}[[H, S], S] + \cdots.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}H_eff = U H U^\dagger = e^{-S} H e^{S}. \label{eq: complete time independent rotation}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-039: As stated, the goal is to then choose $S$ such that the block off-diagonal coupling between the the $\{|L\rangle\}$ and $\{|H\rangle\}$ subspaces vanishes up to desired order.
elements.sent-2411-040: It is then necessary to split the unitary generator into its perturbation series $S = \sum_{j=1 S^{(j)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-041: With this we perform a second order transformation by writing \begin{equation}\nonumber H_eff \approx \sum_{i=0}^2H^{(i)} + \sum_{j=1}^2 V^{(j)} + \nonumber + [H^{(0)}, S^{(1)}] + [H^{(0)}, S^{(2)}] + [H^{(1)}, S^{(1)}] + + [V^{(1)}, S^{(1)}] + \frac{1}{2}[[H^{(0)}, S^{(1)}], S^{(1)}]. \label{eq: expanded H_tilde}\end{equation} Equation~(\ref{eq: expanded H_tilde ) provides the necessary conditions for a block-diagonalization of $\mathcal{H}$ up to second order.}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\nonumber H_eff \approx \sum_{i=0}^2H^{(i)} + \sum_{j=1}^2 V^{(j)} + \nonumber + [H^{(0)}, S^{(1)}] + [H^{(0)}, S^{(2)}] + [H^{(1)}, S^{(1)}] + + [V^{(1)}, S^{(1)}] + \frac{1}{2}[[H^{(0)}, S^{(1)}], S^{(1)}]. \label{eq: expanded H_tilde}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-042: These are then usually established in two mathematically equivalent ways.
elements.sent-2411-043: The first is to work on a operator level and solve the operator equations \begin{equation}[H^{(0)}, S^{(1)}] = -V^{(1)} \equiv P^{(1)}, \label{eq: equation to solve s1} [H^{(0)}, S^{(2)}] = -V^{(2)} - [H^{(1)}, S^{(1)}]\equiv P^{(2)}. \label{eq: equation to solve s2}\end{equation} These equations are often solved via heuristic approaches by first guessing the form of $S^{(j)}$ based on the form of the perturbation $P^{(j)}$.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}[H^{(0)}, S^{(1)}] = -V^{(1)} \equiv P^{(1)}, \label{eq: equation to solve s1} [H^{(0)}, S^{(2)}] = -V^{(2)} - [H^{(1)}, S^{(1)}]\equiv P^{(2)}. \label{eq: equation to solve s2}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-044: This method allows us to retain the operator character of the SWT approach; however, for complicated perturbations, guessing the correct form of $S^{(j)}$ is often too difficult of a task.
elements.sent-2411-045: The second method usually adapted for solving for $S^{(j)}$ is to relate it to the inverse energy difference between the low and high-energy states, effectively capturing the perturbative nature of the interaction.
elements.sent-2411-046: This is often achieved by considering the matrix elements $\bra{L S^{(j)}\ket{H}$ of $S^{(j)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Explicitly, for a small perturbation of order $j$, $S^{(j)}$ is computed as \begin{equation}\bra{L}S^{(j)}\ket{H} = \frac{\bra{L}P^{(j)}\ket{H}}{E_L - E_H}, \label{eq: sum over indices approach}\end{equation} where $\bra{L P^{(j)}\ket{H}$ represents the matrix elements of the perturbation of order $j$ coupling the low-energy state $\ket{L}$ to the high-energy state $\ket{H}$, while $ E_H $, $ E_L $ are the corresponding unperturbed energies.}
elements.sent-2411-048: This approach for solving for $S^{(j)}$ is often employed when treating complex Hamiltonians, as it allows for a closed formalism to establish the matrix elements of $S^{(j)}$.
elements.sent-2411-049: However, this methodology has two main issues: firstly, this approach loses the operator character of the SWT approach, limiting us to work with matrix elements and energy corrections of the effective Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-050: Furthermore, this technique is especially problematic when treating infinite dimensional Hilbert spaces, as this approach requires an inherent truncation of the inifinite dimensional subspace.
elements.section-2411-003: Universal Solution to the $ S $ Generator
elements.sent-2411-051: In this section, we derive a universal expression for the $ S $ generator that is independent of the Hamiltonian's complexity and dimensionality of the system's Hilbert space, without requiring any dimensional truncation.
elements.sent-2411-052: For the sake of clarity, we restrict our analysis to a class of Hamiltonian systems whose total Hilbert space $\mathds{H}$ can be decomposed as $\mathds{H = \mathds{H _f \otimes \mathds{H _b$, where $\mathds{H _f$ is a finite-dimensional Hilbert space of dimension $d_f$, and $\mathds{H _b$ is a bosonic subspace with an associated number operator $ N \equiv a^\dagger a $.}}}}}

elements.section-2411-004: Dispersive shift in anharmonic, time dependent systems
elements.sent-2411-089: In this section, we apply the methods developed in the earlier sections to analyze a system composed of a two-level system (TLS) with frequency $\Omega_z$, coupled to an anharmonic resonator with frequency $\Omega_T$, characterized by an anharmonicity $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-090: The interaction between these two systems is mediated by a periodic, time-dependent coupling with frequency $\Omega$.

elements.sent-2411-120: In summary this article introduces a novel, systematic approach to the SWT, a perturbation theory extensively used in quantum mechanics to simplify the study of Hamiltonians by decoupling low and high-energy subspaces.
elements.sent-2411-121: This new methodology offers a general solution for constructing effective Hamiltonians without the need for heuristic assumptions or the truncation of Hilbert spaces, making it applicable to both finite, infinite-dimensional systems and their combinations.
elements.sent-2411-122: The framework is also extended to accommodate time-dependent systems, for which we derived a solution for time-periodic perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-123: To exemplify the utility of the approach, we have applied it here to the case of a TLS coupled via a time-periodic term to an anharmonic resonator.
elements.sent-2411-124: We have extracted the N-dependent dispersive shift, demonstrating the power of the treatment to get analytical insight.
elements.sent-2411-125: We hope that this integrated method, combining previously established techniques found in the literature, will prove to be a highly versatile and effective tool for the study of otherwise complicated quantum mechanical systems.
elements.sent-2411-126: Additionally, exploiting the modular nature of the presented solutions, we implemented these results in a systematic software library~\cite{SymPT .}
elements.sent-2411-127: This software takes advantage of the presented general solution to be superior both in applicability and easeness of use to existing libraries~\cite{pymablock.}
